value: 5–10 min – combine sources (RAG) and depth (Deep research) in two prompts.
**Benefit:** In 5–10 min you'll combine **RAG** (answer from sources) and **Deep research** (depth) – two prompts, one topic. Sources + depth in one mini-project.

**What to do:**
👉 Pick a topic that matters to you. Copy the two-prompt template below. Run **prompt 1** (RAG) – get an answer with sources. Then **prompt 2** (Deep research) – paste the first answer or say "based on the previous answer" and get an extended analysis.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)

**Don't aim for perfect** – any topic that's useful to you.
Two-step template – first RAG, then Deep research. Replace [topic] with yours.

PROMPT 1 (RAG):
Analyse [topic]. Use only reliable sources (Eurostat, official portals, research papers). Give your answer with links for each conclusion. If information is missing – say I don't know.

PROMPT 2 (Deep research):
Based on the previous answer, extend the analysis. What are the 3 main gaps? What do the latest (2025–2026) studies say? Provide links.

• Does the answer **include links** to sources?
• Did **prompt 2** add new insights (depth)?
• Could you **use this at work** or in study?
• Were there things the AI **couldn't** answer – and is that clear?

If 2 or more "no" – try another topic or refine the prompt (e.g. a more specific [topic]).

**3 steps:** (1) RAG – query with source requirements (Perplexity, ChatGPT Browse, etc.). (2) Deep research – follow-up question based on the first answer (sub-questions, synthesis). (3) Check – do links work, are conclusions logical?

**Self-reflection:** Did you find reliable sources? Did the follow-up question add new insights? These questions help you prepare for the Module 5 test and Module 6 project.
Do you get it? 5 questions – quick check
How does Deep research differ from a simple RAG query?
Deep research – multi-step process (sub-questions, search, synthesis); RAG is often one query → one answer

You've got it – the difference between one query (RAG) and multi-step analysis (Deep research). If wrong – remember: Deep research = sub-questions + intermediate conclusions + final synthesis. In practice that means deeper but longer. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), block "What is Deep research?"
Which tools offer Deep research–style features?

Good – you know the tools. Perplexity Research, ChatGPT Browse/ADA, Claude with tool use, Gemini all support multi-step research. If wrong – no problem: the main thing is knowing such tools exist and trying at least one. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), block "Where to find it?"
What are the main steps of Deep research?

Deep research differs from simple RAG because the AI breaks the question into sub-questions, searches, draws intermediate conclusions and only then gives a final answer. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), "Link to RAG" block.
When is it worth using Deep research instead of simple RAG?
When the question is complex and you need information from several sources or perspectives
When you need a simple fact (e.g. a date or number)
When you want a faster answer

Deep research fits complex, multi-step questions. For a simple fact, RAG is enough. Rule: if the question can be answered in one sentence – RAG; if you need to analyse, compare, summarise – Deep research. If wrong – see slide "Deep research" (4.3), "Task for the agent" block.
What does "prompt sequence" mean in a Deep research context?
Several related prompts where each builds on the previous result (CoT, ToT)
Several unrelated prompts on different topics

A prompt sequence (Chain-of-Thought, Tree-of-Thought) means each step builds on the result of the previous one. That helps the AI "think" more consistently and give a deeper answer. If wrong – see slide "Practical tasks: prompt sequences" (4.3a).

RAG and Deep research – 7 prompt types progression is complete.
System prompt – main character: tone, reasoning, reactions.
Master prompt – biography and context: where you're from, who you work for.
Process prompt – journey plan from first to last step.
Method prompt – give methodology and steps.
Agent prompt – run the workflow, search, use tools.
RAG prompts – answers from specified sources and documents.
Combo prompts – combine several methods in one prompt.
Tokens, context window, max_tokens, cost – how to shorten and keep quality
Token = smallest unit of text the AI processes. Context window and max_tokens limit how much information "fits". 7 tips – how to shorten and keep quality. Practice below.
A token is the smallest unit of text the AI processes. Roughly: 1 token ≈ 4 characters (English) or ~0.75 words. In other languages tokens can be shorter (e.g. with accents).
The sentence "Artificial intelligence is changing the job market" ≈ 7 tokens (English). 1 A4 page (~2000 characters) ≈ 500–600 tokens. A whole book (~80,000 words) ≈ ~100,000 tokens.

The maximum input and output text (in tokens) the model can "see" at once. When exceeded – the oldest information is lost (the model "forgets" the start of the conversation). Includes: your query + chat history + generated response.

1) **Structure** – split topics into sub-topics (shorter conversations). 2) **Shorten** – precise, short prompts save tokens. 3) **Summarise** – in long chats ask for a summary. 4) **Limit output** – use max_tokens, "max 150 words", "list only". 5) **Templates** – system prompt with fixed instructions so you don't repeat.
Longer context = higher cost and slower response. A short, clear prompt with clear Output limits – saves money and time. E.g. "No intro – answer only" or "Give a numbered list only (5 items)".
In long conversations the AI loses focus – three mechanisms and three practices

**Context degradation** – why the AI "forgets" in long conversations. Three mechanisms: Lost in the Middle, attention spread, context displacement (FIFO). Three practices: checkpoint, separate chats, repeat priorities.
What happens under the hood: 3 context-loss mechanisms

**1. Lost in the Middle** – models absorb information best at the **start** or **end** of the text; information in the **middle** loses impact (research: accuracy can drop from >90% to ~40%).

**2. Attention spread** – as the number of tokens grows, the model's "attention" to each specific fact decreases.

**3. Context displacement (FIFO)** – when the technical limit is reached, the oldest information is dropped to make room for new input.
How an important priority gets lost in a long conversation – and what risk that is for the business.
**100% accuracy.** Model fully focuses on this value.
Cost-cutting options, logistics, supplier contracts discussed.
**70% accuracy.** Main priority starts to "drown" in technical detail.
Manager asks: "Which logistics plan is best?"
**Risk:** The model may suggest the cheapest option, which sharply increases workload for staff, **forgetting** the priority stated in the 1st message.

"The model's ability to identify a specific fact in long context decreases exponentially when that fact is not directly related to the latest query." — *Liu et al., Stanford University (2023)*

• **Loss of effectiveness:** In complex sessions, consistency of reasoning drops on average **~39%** over a long discussion.
• **Error accumulation:** A wrong assumption in the 5th message becomes "fact" by the 15th (hallucination chain).

**1. Checkpoint method** – every ~10 messages ask the AI to summarise what we've agreed.

**2. Isolate context** – use separate chat windows for different projects.

**3. Repeat priorities** – restate the most important rules in each critical query (Prompt re-injection).

**Try it:** Start your next long conversation with a 1–2 sentence summary – that reduces "forgetting" risk.

**Stanford / UC Berkeley (2023):** "Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts" – how models use long context; U-shaped curve (best at start and end).

**DeepMind Research:** "Long-range Language Modeling and Attention Decay" – attention decay in long contexts.

**OpenAI Technical Reports:** "GPT-4o Context Window Management and Limits" – context window management and limits.
Do you get it? 3+ questions – quick check
The maximum input and output text (in tokens) the model can see at once

You've got it – the context window is how many tokens the model "sees" at once. Beyond that limit, the oldest information is lost. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "What is the context window?"
How do you optimise prompts for tokens?
Shorten context, clear Output limits (max words), max_tokens
Good – you know the main token-saving principles. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "7 tips" or "5 tips for token budget" block.
What happens when prompt and answer tokens exceed the context window?
The oldest information is lost (the model "forgets" the start of the conversation)
The model runs slower but loses nothing
The model automatically picks a stronger model
Nothing – the context window is unlimited
The context window is limited. When exceeded, the model "forgets" the oldest parts. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "Context window" block.
Roughly how many characters is 1 token (English)?
In English, 1 token ≈ 4 characters or ~0.75 words. That matters when planning how much text "fits" in the context window. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "What is a token?"
What is the max_tokens parameter used for?
To limit the length of the AI response (and control cost)

max_tokens limits how many tokens the AI can generate in the response. That helps control cost and keep the answer from being too long. E.g. max_tokens=500 ≈ ~375 words. If wrong – see slide "Token economy" (4.4), "Technical parameters" or "7 tips" block.
Next – slide 37: Negative prompts (what to avoid)
What to avoid and how to rewrite – vague, too short, biased prompts; examples from best practice
You'll learn to spot **negative prompts** (too vague, too short, no goal, biased) and rewrite them into effective ones – with a goal, context, and clear output.

**Task:**
👉 Take one of your old or "bad" AI queries (or make up a typical one: "help with a report", "write something") and paste it into the prompt below.

**CTA:** 🔘 Copy prompt (below)

Don't aim for perfect – use what you actually write or wrote.
One prompt – the AI will help you rewrite your query into a clear, controllable version (goal, context, result).

ROLE: You are a prompt-engineering assistant helping to improve queries.

TASK:
Rewrite my query below into a "good" prompt following best practices:
- Clear goal (what I want to get)
- Context (who for, where, what constraints)
- Clear output (format, length, what counts as a good result)
- No vague terms ("help", "optimise", "improve") without concrete actions

CONTEXT (my current / bad query):
[Paste your query here]

RULES:
- Keep my intent but make the query actionable and verifiable
- If information is missing – say what needs to be added
- Give 2 versions: short (1–2 sentences) and longer (with blocks)

OUTPUT FORMAT:
"Short version:" + "Longer version:" with clear blocks.

Check the rewritten prompt:

• Is it **clear** what I want (goal)?
• Is there **context** (who for, where, limits)?
• Is **every word** actionable, not abstract ("analysis", "optimise" without actions)?
• Do I **know** when the result is good (format, length)?

If 2 or more "no" – go back to CONTEXT, add to your original query, then run the prompt again.

**What is a "negative" prompt?** A query that gives unstable or useless results: too vague ("help"), too short (no context), biased (pushing one answer), or full of abstract words without actions ("optimise", "improve"). Best practice: goal + context + clear output + concrete action words.
"Role: analyst. Goal: 1-page summary on [topic] with 3 conclusions. Audience: managers. Format: bullet list, English."
"Write a 3-paragraph intro to [topic] for an article. Tone: accessible, no jargon. Max 150 words."
"Shorten this text to 100 words. Keep the 3 main points. Purpose: email to client."
"Compare [our product] with 2 competitors on [criteria]. Give a table with strengths and weaknesses. Role: independent analyst."
Main Module 4 terms – RAG, Deep research, method/agent prompt, token, hallucination, etc.
Specifies how to do the task – role, steps, format; the model generates the answer from the methodology without needing external search or tools (e.g. analysis report).
Asks the AI to perform actions – search, use tools (Browse, Search, API), gather data; the AI acts as an agent; needs a platform with tool/search support (e.g. "search and find TOP10").
Retrieval-Augmented Generation – a method where the AI generates the answer using retrieved information from documents or databases, not only from its training.
In-depth research with AI – multiple steps (splitting questions, searching sources, synthesis into one answer or report).
The smallest unit of text the AI processes; roughly ~4 characters (EN); determines context window and cost.
The maximum input and output text (in tokens) the model can "see" at once; when exceeded, the oldest information is lost.
Using strategically worded queries to influence AI answers (bias, leading questions, etc.); what to avoid – neutral prompts.
AI-generated content that looks convincing but is not fact-based, inaccurate or made up; the model "fills gaps" with probabilities.
In the 6-block system – quality requirements: sources, citation, "I don't know" rule, objectivity, limits.
Chain of thought – asking the AI to lay out its reasoning step by step before giving the conclusion.
Tree of thought – several parallel branches of reasoning or options, from which the best is chosen.
Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback – learning from human feedback; used to shape "better" answer strategies in AI models.
A personalised prompt about the user or organisation (who you are, what you do, goals, style, audience), giving the AI context; differs from the system prompt, which defines how the AI should behave.
Instructions to the AI system on how it should behave – rules, limits, behaviour, format (not about the user, but about the model's role and limits).
